message.content:
The shop smelled of cedar shavings, brass polish, and the faint, metallic tang of old oil. Dust motes danced in the single shaft of afternoon sunlight that pierced the gloom, illuminating hundreds of ticking faces.

Seven-year-old Leo stood on a stool, his chin resting on the glass counter. Before him sat Elias, a man whose fingers were as gnarled as the roots of an oak tree, yet moved with impossible delicacy. He was holding a tiny gear no larger than a lentil between tweezers.

"Mr. Elias?" Leo whispered, afraid to disturb the symphony of ticks.

Elias didn’t look up. "Yes, Leo?"

"Why do they all tick at different speeds? The grandfather clock in the hall goes *tock... tock...* but the little silver one on your wrist goes *tick-tick-tick-tick*."

Elias finally looked up, his eyes magnified behind thick spectacles. He smiled, a slow cracking of dry earth. "Ah. You’ve noticed the heartbeat of the world."

"Is time fast or slow?" Leo asked, leaning forward. "My mom says time flies when I’m playing. But when I’m waiting for the cake to bake, it’s super slow. Is time broken?"

Elias chuckled, a sound like grinding gears. "Time isn’t broken, Leo. It’s just... indifferent. It doesn’t care if you’re playing or waiting. It flows like a river. But *you* are the boat. When you’re having fun, you’re sailing with the current, so it feels fast. When you’re bored, you’re fighting the current, so it feels slow."

Leo frowned, processing this. "So time is like water?"

"In a way," Elias said, setting the gear down. He picked up a small, intricate pocket watch
